rulings.Case Title: RONALD RAMM et al VS. ASBESTOS DEFENDANTS (B*P)AS REFLECTED ON EXHIBITS et al
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, JANUARY 29, 2008 LINE 15. DEFENDANT GRINNELL CORPORATION'S, NOW KNOWN AS GRINNELL, L.L.C. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT IS DENIED. Defendant has shifted the burden of proof in that plaintiff's response to defendant's "state all facts" interrogatories does not provide a basis for linking plaintiff to an asbestos product supplied by defendant. In response, plaintiff's declaration is more precise. It provides evidence that defendant supplied transite pipe, pipe containing asbestos, in the 1960's and 1970's where plaintiff worked installing sprinkler systems. In doing so, plaintiff cut the pipe. Plaintiff also testified that he worked for Grinnell in the late 60's and early 70's. What you have is an installer of sprinkler systems, cutting pipe during an era when transite pipe contained asbestos and where defendant was a regular supplier of pipe to plaintiff's job site. Plaintiff's evidence also contains an admission that certain of its branches in California supplied Johns Manville asbestos pipe. This circumstantial evidence is sufficient to create a triable issue of fact and is stronger than the evidence found to be inadequate in McConnell c. Kaiser Gypsum Co. (2002) 98 Cal.App.4th 1098, 1105. Plaintiff's testimony regarding steel pipe and his declaration raise possible issues of credibility but the deposition testimony is not so definitive as to invoke D'Amico. The failure to warn issue is linked to the product identification issue and for that reason, summary adjudication is denied. =(302/PJM/AY)

rulings.Case Title: FIRST, LAST & ALWAYS VS. SMITH & FONG COMPANY, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion Contesting The Good Faith Of The Settlement Between Mattingly-Thaler Architure And Mitchell Kapor And Freada Klein
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, JANUARY 29, 2008 LINE 5. DEFENDANT SMITH & FONG COMPANY'S Motion Contesting The Good Faith Of The Settlement Between Mattingly-Thaler ARCHITECTURE And Mitchell Kapor And Freada Klein IS DENIED. THE DECISION IN GOVIS ENGINEERING V SUPERIOR COURT (1995) 37 CAL. APP. 4TH 642, 651 DEMONSTRATES THAT THE PARTIES' ALLOCATION IS NOT RES JUDICATA IN A SUBSEQUENT ACTION BETWEEN THE MANUFACTURER AND OWNER. FURTHER, THE EVIDENCE REFLECTS THAT THE HARDWOOD SUPPLIED FELL FAR BELOW THE SPECIFIED HARDNESS. AS A CONSEQUENCE, IT WAS NOT THE ARCHITECT'S SPECIFICATIONS THAT ARE AT FAULT, BUT THE PRODUCT THAT WAS MANUFACTURED. =(302/PJM/VC)
